exemplar.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

detailedExemplarData.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables are fully labelled with units, uncertainties, means and processed quantities, ensuring clarity and precision.
Graphs include error bars, best-fit lines, and correct significant figures throughout calculations.
Processing steps (mean calculation, linear regression, uncertainty propagation to Î¼â, percentage error) are executed logically and accurately.
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation uses a simplistic max-min method and omits current uncertainty in slope propagation.
Graph presentation lacks RÂ² values and gridlines to support precision assessment.
Spatial systematic errors (sensor orientation/positioning) are not propagated into the uncertainty analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is both clear and precise, meeting the highest descriptor. Data processing is accurate with only minor numerical issues. Consideration of uncertainties is appropriate but includes significant omissions in statistical treatment. A score of 5 reflects strong overall performance with room for deeper uncertainty analysis.

strengths: The research question is embedded in a specific, theory-linked context (AmpÃ¨reâs law, solenoid geometry, apparatus details).
Materials list and step-by-step procedure include component values, uncertainties and exact setups, supporting reproducibility.
Variable definitions and control tables are clearly presented with uncertainties, enabling another student to replicate the investigation.
weaknesses: Notation error in Equation 1 (cross product instead of dot product for AmpÃ¨reâs law line integral).
Variable labels are swapped (I and l definitions reversed), causing potential confusion.
Orientation of the Pasco 3-axis sensor is not specified, creating a minor ambiguity in data collection.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands achieve top-band performance with only minor omissions. The context is specific and detailed, methodological descriptions are sufficient for full reproducibility, and considerations are well explained. Small notational and labelling errors prevent the maximum score of 6, so a score of 5 appropriately reflects the quality and minor areas for improvement.

strengths: The conclusion explicitly links experimental results (B â I and slope determination of Î¼â) to the graphical analysis.
Comparison of the experimental Î¼â with the accepted value from the Data Booklet quantifies systematic error.
weaknesses: Limited scientific context: impact of the iron coreâs relative permeability is not discussed.
Proportionality is asserted without statistical testing (e.g., confidence interval or hypothesis test).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clearly supported by the analysis and includes a relevant comparison to literature, aligning with the middle-high band. However, the discussion of scientific context is superficial and lacks deeper theoretical insight and statistical justification, limiting the score to 4.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (ambient fields, inrush current, core length) are identified and their directional impacts on results are explained.
Recommended improvements (magnetically quiet room, wait times, longer core, variable resistor) are realistic and directly linked to the limitations.
weaknesses: Quantitative estimation of each errorâs relative contribution is missing.
Discussion of field uniformity limitation is left incomplete; impact magnitude is not elaborated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation explains the relative impact of specific weaknesses and proposes realistic, relevant improvements, fulfilling top-band requirements. The lack of quantitative estimates and an incomplete thought prevent a maximum score, so a 5 accurately reflects the strong but not perfect evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question â the relationship between current in a 60-turn solenoid and the magnetic field it produces â is clearly stated and immediately embedded in the well-developed discussion of AmpÃ¨reâs law and the specific geometry (core radius 1 cm, length 15 cm).  By linking the question to explicit theory, safety considerations and the exact apparatus to be used, the student establishes a specific and appropriate context rather than a broad or generic one.  Minor omissions (e.g. no broader real-world application) stop the work short of being exemplary, so a high score of 5 in the top band is appropriate.

Instrumental uncertainties are stated, error bars drawn and propagation carried through to the final value of Î¼â.  However, the method for averaging uncertainty (simple mean division) is not statistically sound, and current uncertainty is not propagated into the slope.  These significant omissions prevent a top-band award but exceed the limited treatment of the 1â2 band, meriting 4 in the 3â4 band.

The student explains why five current settings and five repeats were chosen (precision, safety), justifies controlled variables and identifies environmental magnetic fields as a concern.  These comments move beyond merely listing controls: they explain how each consideration secures data that are both relevant and sufficient to answer the question.  Some factors (e.g. limited current range) could have been anticipated in the design stage rather than the evaluation, so the strand earns 5 rather than 6.

Specific weaknessesâambient magnetic fields, inrush current, short solenoid, absence of vacuumâare identified and the direction of their impact on the results is clearly explained (e.g., raising B readings, inflating Î¼â).  The causal links show understanding of relative impact, meeting the top-band descriptor; a minor lack of quantitative estimation of each effect keeps the score at 5 rather than 6.

The step-by-step procedure lists component values, cable connections, sensor orientation and exact distances (1.00 cm Â± 0.05 cm), together with the five voltage settings that generate the stated currents.  This level of detail is sufficient for another student to replicate the experiment without clarification.  Minor ambiguities (e.g. which axis of the 3-axis sensor is used) prevent a perfect 6, but the description still meets the descriptor that reproduction is possible.

Data processing (mean values, linear regression, max/min gradients, propagation of uncertainty to Î¼â, percentage error) is executed logically and with few numerical errors.  Units are retained and the final comparison to literature is consistent.  A minor issueâpotential unit slip when converting mA to Aâappears to have been handled implicitly, so a score of 5 (strong performance within the top band) is appropriate.

Improvements are realistic (use magnetically quiet room, wait for current stabilisation, longer core, variable resistor, repeated current measurements) and each is tied to a specific weakness with an explanation of how it would lower error or expand the range.  The explanations are detailed but not quantified, supporting a strong score of 5 in the top band rather than the maximum 6.

Raw data are organised in a labelled table with units and absolute uncertainties, means are calculated, and processed quantities (slope, Î¼â) are presented systematically.  Graphs carry axis labels, error bars and multiple best-fit lines.  Calculations are laid out clearly with correct significant figures.  Both clarity and precision are consistently maintained, fully satisfying the top-band descriptor.

The work compares the experimental Î¼â to the accepted value, quantifies a 49 % error and discusses the expected zero intercept.  While relevant, the comparison is limited to a single constant and does not extend to deeper theoretical discussion (e.g., influence of iron core permeability) that would provide fuller justification.  This aligns with the middle-high band, earning 4.

The student draws a clear, data-based conclusion: B â I, slope gives Î¼â, and the non-zero intercept indicates systematic error.  These statements are explicitly linked to the processed graph and numerical analysis, fulfilling the requirement that the conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis.  A perfect 6 is withheld because the proportionality is asserted rather than demonstrated through statistical testing.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly embedded in a specific context, linking AmpÃ¨reâs law, solenoid geometry, and apparatus details, enhancing reproducibility.

Equation 1 uses a cross product notation (\(\vec B \times d\vec l\)) instead of the standard dot product for a line integral. Correct to \(\vec B\cdot d\vec l\) to align with AmpÃ¨reâs law notation.
Consider mentioning a real-world application of solenoids (e.g., MRI, electromagnets) to deepen the background context and link theory to practice.
The variable definitions incorrectly state that \(I\) represents length. Swap labels so \(I\) denotes current and \(l\) denotes solenoid length to avoid confusion.
Table 1 provides detailed independent, dependent, and control variables with uncertainties, enabling reproducibility of the experimental setup.
Clarify which axis of the Pasco 3-axis sensor is used to measure the field; specifying orientation avoids ambiguity in data collection.
The paragraph after Table 1 duplicates control variable details; consider removing or integrating it to streamline the presentation.
The materials list is comprehensive, including all sensors, wires, and measurement uncertainties, supporting clear experimental design.
The step-by-step procedure provides sufficient detail (wire wrapping, connections, measurement steps) for replication with minimal ambiguity.
You note âno environmental or ethical concernsââcould there be disposal issues or data integrity considerations? Reflect further on potential ethical/environmental impacts.
Uncertainties for current (Â±0.1 mA) and field (Â±0.001 mT) are clearly stated, ensuring transparent error reporting.
Propagate uncertainty arising from sensor orientation and positioning, not just device resolution, to capture systematic spatial errors.
Convert current values to amperes on the data table for consistency with SI units used in equations, avoiding unit conversion errors.
Inclusion of a âÂ±1â uncertainty for turn count demonstrates awareness of manufacturing error, strengthening uncertainty analysis.
Enhance the graph in Figure 12 by including gridlines, clear axis units, and an RÂ² value to aid precision assessment.
Slope calculation uses correct significant figures and clear arithmetic layout, supporting accurate determination of \(\mu_0\).
The use of \((\text{max slope}-\text{min slope})/2\) for uncertainty is simplistic; consider statistical treatment (standard error) for more rigorous propagation.
Calculation of \(\mu_0\) applies propagation formulas correctly, integrating slope, length, and turn count uncertainties appropriately.
Current uncertainty was omitted in slope propagation; incorporate it into \(\delta\mu_0/\mu_0\) for complete uncertainty analysis.
The percentage error calculation is clear; extend the scientific context by discussing the impact of iron core permeability on the deviation.
The conclusion explicitly states direct proportionality and links to the graph, ensuring consistency between data and interpretation.
The conclusion asserts proportionality without a statistical test (e.g., confidence interval around slope); include such analysis to strengthen justification.
Comparing the experimental \(\mu_0\) with the IB Physics Data Booklet value is relevant and supports the discussion of systematic error.
Expand the scientific context comparison by discussing how the iron coreâs relative permeability affects the theoretical \(\mu\).
The evaluation pinpoints ambient magnetic fields and explains their upward effect on readings, demonstrating strong grasp of systematic bias.
Identifying inrush current as a possible error and proposing extra waiting time demonstrates thoughtful consideration of procedure improvement.
The discussion of relative uncertainties is descriptive but lacks quantification of their impact on final \(\mu_0\); include percentage contributions for clarity.
When addressing random error reduction, quantify how many additional measurements are needed to halve uncertainty for targeted precision.
The limitation regarding field uniformity is identified but the sentence is incomplete; finish the thought on its potential measurement impact.
Considering comparison with absolute permeability (\(\mu=\mu_0\mu_r\)) rather than free-space value would yield a more valid evaluation of method accuracy.
The clear discussion of variable control as a strength underscores sound design and effective reduction of confounding factors.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
